# **From Cursive to Code: How AI Turned Our Family Recipe Book into a Treasure for Generations**

[![Adrian](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*O2j7NIvOB54-5f97HCMeiA.png)](/?source=post_page---byline--fe94cc0cf206---------------------------------------)

[Adrian](/?source=post_page---byline--fe94cc0cf206---------------------------------------)

5 min read

·

May 29, 2025

--

--

[Listen](https://medium.com/m/signin?actionUrl=https%3A%2F%2Fmedium.com%2Fplans%3Fdimension%3Dpost_audio_button%26postId%3Dfe94cc0cf206&operation=register&redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fadrianei.medium.com%2Ffrom-cursive-to-code-how-ai-turned-our-family-recipe-book-into-a-treasure-for-generations-fe94cc0cf206&source=---header_actions--fe94cc0cf206---------------------post_audio_button------------------)

Share

There’s a certain kind of magic in a smudged recipe card.
The faded ink, the curling edges, the unmistakable whiff of cinnamon trapped in the fibers — it’s more than just paper. It’s memory. History. Family.

My mother’s family recipe book was one of those treasures — passed around for over 50 years, written in a swirl of beautiful but stubborn cursive. It was filled with substitutions, side notes (“use less salt!”, “This one was good!” scrawled by Mom in the margins), and the occasional chocolate fingerprint. It lived in a drawer, then a box, then a cupboard — only sometimes emerging to bask in a patch of sunlight on the countertop, its pages gently curling at the edges, quietly holding together generations of shared meals and fading memories.

Until now.

This year, something extraordinary happened. I gave the book to AI.
Not to replace it — but to revive it.

![](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:624/1*PReMVc0CUGAx4L1-bn3ZLw.png)

**The Flavor of Memory: Why Food Connects Us Like Nothing Else**

A few months ago, I invited my cousin and his family over for Friday night dinner. I took the liberty of preparing a couple of traditional dishes my late grandmother used to make: *Perogen* (a South African variation of the traditional Eastern European pierogi meat pie) and fish cakes in apricot curry sauce — one of the very few truly spicy dishes Gran reserved for holidays and special occasions. A real treat for those with good taste 😄.

I didn’t do it to impress, but to share in a moment of reminiscence. To enjoy a dish with family who I knew would appreciate it — seasoned not only with herbs but with memories more potent than the spices themselves.
(Needless to say, these dishes went completely wasted on my kids, who would sooner choose bland pasta with ketchup over anything else.)

That evening, I got a whole lot more than I bargained for.
 My cousin’s first words as he walked through the door into the warmly-lit dining room:
 **“Oh my G-D! It smells like Granny Frida’s kitchen!”** he said with excitement.

In a matter of seconds, I was taken back to our childhood — playing on Gran’s carpeted lounge while our parents shared the events of their day with Granny Frida and Grandpa Natie. Hiding from my older brothers behind the cream-colored couches. Peering out at the street below, where the parked cars glimmered like stars in the pitch-black night.

In that brief moment, I realized two things:

* How much I missed my grandparents, and what I wouldn’t give to sit down with them for one more meal…
* What fantastic power these recipes had!

**The Problem with Paper (and the Power of AI)**

The book had become almost unreadable. Much of it was handwritten, and not just in neat schoolteacher cursive. No — this was the loopy, rushed, lovingly chaotic handwriting of my mother. Recipes scribbled haphazardly, meticulously, and sometimes in a burst of energy so as not to miss a single ingredient being dictated to her by my gran.

AI stepped in, and with its remarkable ability to read and transcribe cursive, it translated all those ink swirls into clean, searchable text.
Every stubborn “f” mistaken for a “t”, every “1/2” written like “12” — it fixed them. Patiently. Precisely.
Like a grandchild carefully listening to their grandmother explain a recipe for the third time.

A sample transcription of a recipe to digital form

**Breathing New Life Into Old Recipes**

Once the words were saved, AI generated images — visual interpretations of each recipe.
The matzah ball soup, golden and steaming. The apple cake, dense and cinnamon-dusted. Even the brisket, glistening with that glaze that only appeared once a year on holidays.

For the first time, our family’s food had faces.
Somehow, the digital representations brought the recipes closer.
You could look at a photo of a dish, close your eyes, and the room would fill with the aromatic fragrance slowly dissipating around you — and just a moment, a brief moment I could see Grandpa again, sneaking a second helping before dessert.

**More Than Just Meals**

This wasn’t just a project to digitize a cookbook.
This was preservation. It was legacy.

Every family has a language, and ours is food.
Oven-fresh [Kitke](https://www.dailymaverick.co.za/article/2022-02-25-the-peculiarly-south-african-story-of-kitke) on Friday nights, [Kichel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kichel) & chopped herring.
Soup so full of garlic it could clear a head cold *and* a crowded room.

We talk through our cooking. We remember through it. We celebrate. We mourn. We connect — always — through the meals we make and share.

By turning this old, fragile book into a living digital archive, we’re giving my parents’ sons, daughters-in-law, and grandchildren more than just instructions for baking.
We’re giving them the smells of their childhoods, the comfort of “home,” the taste of traditions they didn’t even know they missed.

This book will live on — not just in a cupboard, but on screens and in kitchens around the world.
It’s already being emailed, printed, bookmarked, and bookmarked again. Recipes are getting tagged and adapted, just like before.
The difference? Now everyone can read them.

**Fifty More Years**

It’s easy to dismiss AI as cold or impersonal. But sometimes, it’s the opposite.
Sometimes, it’s the tool that helps a family stitch together a past that was slipping through their fingers.
It’s the bridge between generations.

It’s how a great-grandmother’s flour-dusted recipe, once barely legible, becomes a dish served at a 10-year-old’s birthday — half a century later.

Our recipe book was never just about food.
It was about people.
It was about family.

And now, thanks to a bit of modern magic, it’s about legacy too.

And we can’t wait to see where — and who — it feeds next.

Meet George Robert Martin III – A Writer Fueled by Passion, Purpose, and Family

Greetings! I’m George Robert Martin III, and I’m not your average writer—I’m a storyteller, innovator, and proud father of three incredible children who inspire me every single day. They are my heart, my drive, and the reason I’ve dedicated myself to creating meaningful, uplifting, and impactful works that resonate with others.

In these challenging economic times, I’ve chosen to channel my creativity and life experiences into authoring short stories, How-To guides, and insightful resources designed not just to entertain, but to empower and uplift readers navigating their own journeys. Writing has always been more than a hobby for me—it’s my art form, my sanctuary, and my way of leaving a legacy.

With years of study, practice, and refinement behind me, I’ve developed a bold, energetic, and authentic writing style that breaks molds and pushes boundaries. Each story, each guide I write is crafted with passion, precision, and a deep desire to connect with readers on an emotional level—whether I’m making them laugh, sparking curiosity, or guiding them through life’s challenges with practical wisdom.

I believe wholeheartedly in the power of storytelling to transform, heal, and inspire. My mission is simple: to share my voice, my vision, and my words with the world, hoping to spark joy, imagination, and hope in every reader who picks up my work.

If my stories touch your heart, make you think, or simply brighten your day, I’d be honored if you’d help spread the word and invite others to join me on this journey. Your support isn’t just appreciated—it’s the lifeblood of this creative dream I’m building for myself, my family, and my readers.

Thank you for being part of this adventure. Here’s to brighter days, unforgettable tales, and the beauty of creation shared. I can’t wait to welcome you into my world of words.

With gratitude and excitement,

George Robert Martin III

  First, congrats on your family Cookbooks. I have self-published a cookbook for my family, consisting of recipes from my great-great-grandmother to my granddaughters, 7 generations in all. I understand what a huge undertaking it is. I wish that I had your capabilities, as I would have loved to have some pictures of each generation. I did take pictures of each recipe as I made them thank goodness for digital! But they do not have the clarity of yours. The only suggestion I would offer is the formatting with multiple pictures of the same thing, and the way your content page was written was very confusing to me. But as that is not important to the quality of the recipes, and the fact that I was able to make them and they were delicious, I would say, congrats on doing a lifelong ambition of many of us. You know, "Us"; the ones who value the recipes handed down from our grandparents, eaten every holiday in Hopefully great grandma's serving dish. My prized possession is handwritten recipes, written in Portuguese, from my great-grandmother of her mother's favorite recipes. My ancestor was eleven years old, and the only one in her family that could write legibility. I did end up typing it into the computer to translate. I also have added some personal stories, so hopefully as they are handed down, they understand that food is love, that you can consume!

Heirloom Kitchen: Heritage Recipes & Family Stories From the Tables of Immigrant Women

## EDIBLE READS – Heirloom Kitchen: Heritage Recipes & Family Stories From the Tables of Immigrant Women

* [By
  Maria Buteux Reade](https://ediblevermont.ediblecommunities.com/author/maria-buteux-reade/)
* |
  November 19, 2020

![edible Reads - Heirloom Kitchen](https://ediblevermont.ediblecommunities.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/LRHeirloom20Kitchen20Cover20281292028129-804x1024.jpg)

HEIRLOOM KITCHEN Heritage Recipes & Family Stories from the Tables of Immigrant Women
Anna Francese Gass, April 2019

“A mother is full when her children have eaten.”

Anna Francese Gass doesn’t remember which woman shared this observation, but the sentiment rings true with anyone who cooks for their loved ones.

Anna spent four years traveling across the United States cooking with 37 women who had immigrated to America from 30 countries in pursuit of a better life for themselves and for their families. “I started with the mothers of my first-generation friends, and it evolved from there,” she explains. Those cooking sessions began as a blog and morphed into her Heirloom Kitchen cookbook.

“I’m the daughter of an Italian immigrant. I grew up in a home where my mom made the same dishes she learned from her own mother. Those are my mother’s hands on the book’s cover photo. She’s cutting tagliatelle just as she saw her mom do in Italy. That photo tells my own heritage story.”

Although a culinary-school trained chef and professional recipe tester, Anna realized she had never taken the time to learn how to make her mom’s dishes. So, she began a family project. “I started cooking with my mom, using my skills to formalize recipes based on her instinctual approach. I learned that a handful translates into a cup, a pinch into a half-teaspoon. Mom prepared the dishes she knew by

heart, and I stood at her elbow taking copious notes. How long did that boil? How much flour did you add? How small did you cut those onions? I’m precise and methodical by nature, but my mom relaxed and embraced the project. She was deeply touched that I cared enough about the meals and process she took for granted.”

Anna says she has always been drawn to people from first-generation families, children who grew up in homes where English was not the primary language. To expand this heirloom recipe project, she reached out to her first-gen friends and asked if she could cook with their mothers. “Much like my mom, only a few had recipes written out. I explained that I would transcribe recipes based on what their moms cooked and gift them back to the family in exchange for the cooking lesson. I was so honored that these women opened their homes and their hearts to me, a complete stranger in most cases.”

As they cooked, each woman shared her immigration story, which Anna crafted into poignant vignettes. “The common threads drew me in. The women had moved to the United States for education, career opportunities, and even safety in some cases. And while readers love the recipes, these immigration stories also resonate. This is more than ‘just’ a cookbook. It’s an oral history anthology, a love letter to our immigrant parents who had the courage to leave behind everything and everyone they knew, to come to the Land of Opportunity. And while they embraced the American Dream, keeping their unique culture alive through food, language, and story remained paramount.”

Flip through the pages and enjoy a global culinary tour. Susanne’s Appelkoken (apple donuts) from Germany. Irene’s Sambousek, a Lebanese cheese hand pie. Shekaiba’s Bolani, Afghani fried potato and scallion turnovers. Jennipher’s Ashanti Fish Stew from Ghana. Rocio’s Pastel de Alcachofas, Peruvian artichoke tart. Monika’s Piernik Storopolski, chocolate-enrobed spice cake, a Polish holiday treat.

And of course, Tagliatelle with Brodo di Mamma e Polpette, offered by Anna’s mother, Gina, and her grandmother, Grazia. A pasta and meatballs in tomato sauce dish that Anna has preserved in words, even though her own children have learned to make it at their grandmother’s elbow.

[![](https://ediblevermont.ediblecommunities.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/R.BonBons.jpg)](/article/slideshow/81554?fid=166250&width=100%25&height=100%25)

BRIGADEIROS BONBONS

**MORGANA—Minas Gerais, Brazil
BRIGADEIROS (CHOCOLATE BONBONS)**
*From Heirloom Kitchen*

Morgana Oliveira grew up in Brazil where she remembers her grandmother preparing traditional meals for the family. Although Morgana immigrated to the United States for college and eventually started a family, she still returns to Brazil each year and maintains cultural traditions so her daughter stays connected to her heritage, including these delicious chocolate bonbons, called brigadeiros, a traditional sweet made for birthday celebrations in Brazil. Morgana explains that there is a strict rule: No sampling from the table of tempting desserts until the birthday song is sung and the cake is cut—no exceptions! Even harder than waiting? Trying to decide which flavor of bonbon is more delicious: the brigadeiro (chocolate) or the beijinho (coconut; variation below).

*Makes about 24 (1-inch) bonbons*

**INGREDIENTS**
1 (14-ounce) can sweetened condensed milk
1/3 cup crème de leite or heavy cream
¼ cup unsweetened cocoa powder, or more to taste
1 cup chocolate sprinkles
1 tablespoon unsalted butter, at room temperature

Heat the condensed milk, crème de liete, and cocoa powder in a medium pan over medium heat and cook until the mixture thickens enough that the spoon leaves a streak along the pan, 12 to 15 minutes. Transfer the mixture to a bowl, cover, and refrigerate for 20 to 30 minutes until completely cool. Pour the sprinkles into a bowl or baking dish. Place 24 mini cupcake liners on a serving plate. Rub a little butter on your hands, then scoop up about a teaspoon of the chocolate mixture and roll it into a 1-inch ball. Roll it in the chocolate sprinkles and place it in a cupcake liner. Repeat for the remaining 23 bonbons. Chill for 30 minutes before serving. Store any leftovers in an airtight container for up to one week.

Variation: Morgana’s Beijinho (Coconut Bonbons) Replace the cocoa powder with 1 cup unsweetened, finely shredded coconut flakes (add more to taste). Follow the steps above, cooking for only 5 minutes in step 1; chill for 15 minutes, to make approximately 30 beijinho.

[![](https://ediblevermont.ediblecommunities.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/11/rChicken.jpg)](/article/slideshow/81554?fid=166252&width=100%25&height=100%25)

CHICKEN TAGINE

**SAFOI—Rabat, Morocco
CHICKEN TAGINE**
*From Heritage Kitchen*

Safoi Babana-Hampton, a professor of Modern French Studies, moved from Morocco to the United States as a Fulbright scholar. To keep Moroccan traditions alive, she fills her home with artifacts that carry historical significance or spiritual value. She believes that “food provides both insight and history of one’s culture.” Her kitchen in Michigan has many beautiful tagines, a cooking vessel used in Moroccan recipes. Made of earthenware with a domed top that returns all condensation to the food, a tagine is perfect for slow-cooked foods such as stews and bone-in chicken dishes. Meat falls off the bone and all the flavors marry together. If you invest in one, you may never use your slow cooker again.

Tagine is also the name of a type of dish commonly served in Morocco. “When I place my tagine on the stove and fire it up, I feel as if I’ve been transported to Morocco.”

Serves 6

**INGREDIENTS**

*For the marinade*

½ teaspoon saffron threads
1 teaspoon ground ginger
1 tablespoon coarse salt
1 teaspoon freshly ground black pepper
½ cup extra-virgin olive oil
½ cup canola oil

*For the stew*

1 small whole chicken
7 large carrots, peeled
1 small yellow onion, minced (about ½ cup) ½ cup olives (Mediterranean or kalamata) ½ preserved lemon, sliced thin
1 tablespoon parsley, chopped

Combine the saffron, ginger, salt, pepper, olive oil, and  canola oil in a large bowl. Set aside. Clean and thoroughly wash the chicken, then cut it into 8 pieces.Add the chicken to the bowl with the marinade, massaging the marinade into the chicken. Cut the carrots in half and remove their yellow cores. Open the tagine and lay the onion on the bottom. Arrange the carrots over the onion. Lay the chicken with all of the marinade over the vegetables. Add 1 cup water and cover. Cook on very low heat for 1 hour.

## Custom-Designed Family Recipe Books & Cookbooks

Family recipe cards are as treasured an heirloom as the family photos and videos we see every day.

## 100,000+

[Home](https://everpresent.com/) » [Custom Services](https://everpresent.com/?page_id=383) » [Photo Book Album Design](https://everpresent.com/services/custom-photobook-design/) » Custom-Designed Family Recipe Books & Cookbooks

What makes our heirloom family recipe books special is not just the recipes themselves, but the visual reminders contained within your scans. Every stain, tear, and fold shows these recipe cards have been well-used and well-loved. Add to this some family photos and annotations and you have a personalized and meaningful compilation for all the family to continue your cooking traditions through the generations.
EverPresent preserves these family memories and our design team uses the latest tools and techniques to bring your project to a new life in print. Invest in your legacy with a breathtaking family heirloom recipe book from our photo book experts.

Our cookbooks were featured in [The Wall Street Journal](https://www.wsj.com/articles/as-recipe-cards-disappear-families-scramble-to-preserve-cherished-memories-11548773745?tesla=y)

![](https://everpresent.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/04/wsj-logo-transparent.png)![](https://everpresent.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/04/wsj-logo-transparent.png)

![](https://everpresent.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/Maczonis-Perms-Remake-Mockup_web-600x450-1.jpg)![](https://everpresent.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/Maczonis-Perms-Remake-Mockup_web-600x450-1.jpg)

## Why choose a service like EverPresent over DIY?

* We have years of experience creating thousands of custom design projects including recipe and cookbooks. We know what to ask, how to organize your materials, and the best ways to showcase and enhance your contents to make the project you’ve been envisioning!
* We can [digitize](https://everpresent.com/digitizing/) any photo, recipe card or clipping, document ,or negative format you want to include
* If your digital photo collections are overwhelming, we offer [digital organizing](https://everpresent.com/organizing-services/digital-photo-organization/) to get you started
* Creating such a unique family heirloom can be a lot of work! Our experts can help pull this project together, giving you one less task to have to figure out.

“I just want to tell you that I just picked up the books and am overwhelmed at how truly beautiful they are. They are works of art and I can’t thank you enough for all the amazing work you did to make them possible.” – Lois W.R.

*Lois’s book was highlighted in the Wall Street Journal feature article. It was a Christmas gift for her sons made up of their family recipes. She didn’t want to add any photos, aside from the one featured on the cover. Since it contained older recipes, we kept the look simple and clean. She included meaningful newspaper clippings from her mother, to capture her spirit along with her recipes for her grandchildren.*

![](data:image/png;base64...)![](https://everpresent.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/cookbook-spread.webp)

[READ OUR BLOG POST ABOUT THIS RECIPE BOOK](https://everpresent.com/turn-old-family-recipes-to-modern-cookbook/)

## Versatile

Our photo books and albums can be customized for any event — birthdays, anniversaries, gifts, commemorations, anything you can think of!

## Personalized

Work directly with your consultant and designer to select your photos, choose your design look, and find the format that best suits your needs.

## Professional

Our expert team includes designers, editors, and photography specialists using industry-standard software to create a beautiful volume just for you.

## Unique

Add scans of memorabilia, custom background photos, typeset memories, and personal dedications to make your book truly unique to your family.

## Cookbooks are not one size fits all:

![](data:image/png;base64...)![](https://everpresent.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/Screenshot-2026-01-10-at-3.57.58-PM-1024x99.png)

### Scanned recipe books

![](data:image/png;base64...)![](https://everpresent.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/cookbook-recipes-300x200-1.jpg)

Recipe books built entirely from your collection of scanned recipe cards and clippings include the recipe cards in their original form, rips, stains and all. If you like, include family photos — particularly those featuring cooking or breaking bread together! Your designer will discuss additional personalization touches with you to make it truly one-of-a-kind.

### Typeset cookbooks

![](data:image/png;base64...)![](https://everpresent.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/cookbook-type-300x200-1.jpg)

If you want to turn your family recipe cards into a fresh new cookbook, you can work with our designers on transcribing the recipe cards into text and using the scanned cards and photos as accents. We will standardize the typesetting of all of your recipes from different sources for consistency and professional polish.

### Recipe “Scrapbook” Remake

![](data:image/png;base64...)![](https://everpresent.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/collected-recipes-300x200-1.jpg)

Oftentimes family recipes are already contained within a book that’s now too fragile to be referenced in the kitchen. We’ll scan the full pages into a sturdy, revitalized cookbook for the entire extended family to share.

### [Travel](https://everpresent.com/services/custom-photobook-design/travel-vacation/)

### Photo Book FAQs

###### How do I choose the recipes?

It’s tough to choose and organize the best photos and recipes for a family cookbook. Your consultant can help pinpoint the recipe clippings, cards, printouts and pictures you want to scan. Bonus points for shots of your family enjoying these meals! Keep the number and size of your scans in mind, too – one recipe might need two separate scans, and larger page scans will take up more space in your cookbook than recipe cards.

###### How does the design process work?

Most of our clients start by having us digitize any photos or documents that aren’t already digital. Meet your consultant and designer to discuss your goals for the project before we begin. We will provide a series of digital drafts for you to review and you may request changes along the way. After you approve the final draft, your book is printed and shipped to you so you can start enjoying it! [For a more detailed overview, visit our general Photo Book design page.](https://everpresent.com/services/custom-photobook-design/)

###### Are photos the only option for my book?

Not at all! Wedding invitations, birth certificates, newspaper clippings, scrapbook pages, letters and cards can be unique add-ons to flesh out your story. We can even photograph 3D objects like awards or plaques in our professional studio.

###### What is the difference between a scanned recipe photo book and a typset cookbook?

A **recipe book** is made up of scanned images of handwritten or typed recipe cards, as well as those favorite clippings and printouts we all have in our collections. With a **typeset cookbook**, we will format and lay out the typed recipes you’ve submitted for a clean, unified, professional cookbook look. You can include photos in both types of books. Please note that due to of the larger amount of typesetting and proofreading required, typeset projects are more time-consuming to create than scanned recipe books.
